questions.question: In Python, which of the following is an immutable data type?
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable — once created their contents cannot be changed. Lists, dicts, and sets are all mutable, which is why only immutable, hashable types like tuples can serve as dictionary keys.

questions.question: Inside a loop, what is the difference between `break` and `continue`?
options: `break` exits the loop entirely; `continue` skips to the next iteration
`break` skips one iteration; `continue` exits the loop
`break` only works in `while` loops; `continue` only in `for` loops
questions.explanation: `break` terminates the entire loop immediately, while `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds to the next one. Both work in `for` and `while` loops.

questions.question: A variable assigned inside a function, without the `global` keyword, has what scope?
options: Global — visible everywhere in the module
Local — visible only inside that function
Nonlocal — visible in the enclosing class
questions.explanation: By default a name bound inside a function is local to that function and is not visible outside it. To modify a module-level variable from inside a function you must declare it with `global`.

questions.question: Which object can be used as a dictionary key?
questions.explanation: Dictionary keys must be hashable, which requires immutability. A tuple of immutable items is hashable; lists, dicts, and sets are mutable and therefore unhashable, so they cannot be keys.

questions.question: Why is the `with open(...) as f:` context-manager pattern preferred for file handling?
options: It makes the file read faster
It automatically closes the file even if an exception occurs
It is the only way to read binary files
It loads the entire file into memory at once
questions.explanation: A context manager guarantees the file's `close()` is called when the block exits — including when an exception is raised — preventing resource leaks. It does not change read speed or force loading the whole file.

questions.question: In object-oriented programming, polymorphism refers to:
options: Hiding an object's internal state behind methods
The ability of different classes to be used through a common interface, each responding in its own way
A class inheriting attributes from a parent class
Bundling data and behaviour into a single unit
questions.explanation: Polymorphism lets objects of different types respond to the same method call in type-specific ways (e.g. each shape's `area()`). Encapsulation is hiding state; inheritance is deriving from a parent.

questions.question: In a try/except/finally block, when does the `finally` clause execute?
options: Only when no exception is raised
Only when an exception is raised
Always, whether or not an exception occurred
Only when the exception is re-raised
questions.explanation: The `finally` block always runs — after the try (and any except) — making it the right place for cleanup such as closing resources, regardless of whether an exception occurred.

questions.question: NumPy 'broadcasting' allows you to:
options: Perform element-wise operations on arrays of different but compatible shapes without explicit loops
Convert a NumPy array to a Python list
Print every element of an array
questions.explanation: Broadcasting stretches smaller arrays across larger ones following shape-compatibility rules, enabling vectorized element-wise math (e.g. adding a scalar or row to a matrix) without writing explicit loops.

questions.question: What is the difference between a pandas Series and a DataFrame?
options: A Series is 2-dimensional; a DataFrame is 1-dimensional
A Series is a single labelled column; a DataFrame is a 2-D table of columns
A Series can only hold numbers; a DataFrame only strings
questions.explanation: A Series is a one-dimensional labelled array (a single column). A DataFrame is a two-dimensional structure — effectively a collection of Series sharing an index — representing tabular data.

questions.question: Which pandas operation groups rows by a column's values and computes an aggregate per group?
questions.explanation: `groupby()` splits the data into groups based on key values, then lets you apply an aggregate (sum, mean, count, …) to each group. `merge` joins, `dropna` removes missing rows, `sort_values` orders.

questions.question: One-hot encoding is used to:
options: Scale numeric features to a 0–1 range
Convert categorical variables into binary indicator columns
questions.explanation: One-hot encoding turns a categorical column into several binary (0/1) columns, one per category, so models that expect numeric input can use the categories without implying a false ordinal relationship.

questions.question: In hypothesis testing, a p-value represents:
options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing data at least as extreme as seen, assuming the null hypothesis is true
The size of the effect being measured
The confidence level of the test
questions.explanation: The p-value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed, under the assumption the null hypothesis holds. A small p-value casts doubt on the null — it is not the probability the null is true.

questions.question: A Type I error in hypothesis testing is:
options: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
Rejecting a true null hypothesis (a false positive)
Choosing too small a sample size
questions.explanation: A Type I error is a false positive — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true. A Type II error is the opposite: failing to reject a null that is false (a false negative).

questions.question: In Matplotlib, what is the relationship between a Figure and an Axes?
options: A Figure is the overall canvas that can hold one or more Axes (individual plots)
They are two names for the same object
An Axes is the x-axis line of a Figure
questions.explanation: The Figure is the top-level container (the whole window/page); each Axes is a single plot with its own data, ticks, and labels. A Figure may hold multiple Axes for subplots.

questions.question: In scikit-learn, why should you call `fit` on the training data only (not the test data) when scaling features?
options: Fitting on test data is computationally slower
To avoid data leakage — test statistics must not influence the transformation
Scikit-learn forbids fitting on test data with an error
It makes no difference which data you fit on
questions.explanation: Fitting a scaler (or any preprocessor) on the test set leaks information about the test distribution into training, inflating performance estimates. You fit on train, then `transform` both train and test with those learned parameters.

questions.question: Compared with Ridge (L2) regression, Lasso (L1) regularization is notable because it can:
options: Drive some feature coefficients exactly to zero, performing feature selection
questions.explanation: L1 (Lasso) penalises the absolute value of coefficients, which can shrink some to exactly zero — effectively selecting features. L2 (Ridge) shrinks coefficients toward zero but rarely makes them exactly zero.

questions.question: The main purpose of k-fold cross-validation is to:
options: Estimate model performance more reliably by training/validating across multiple data splits
Increase the size of the training set
Replace the need for a test set entirely
questions.explanation: K-fold CV partitions data into k folds, training on k-1 and validating on the remaining fold, rotating through all folds. Averaging the results gives a more robust, less variance-prone performance estimate than a single split.

questions.question: What is the difference between a model parameter and a hyperparameter?
options: Parameters are set before training; hyperparameters are learned from data
Parameters are learned from data during training; hyperparameters are set beforehand to control the learning process
Hyperparameters only exist in neural networks
questions.explanation: Parameters (e.g. regression weights) are learned from the data during training. Hyperparameters (e.g. learning rate, number of trees, k in k-NN) are configured before training and govern how learning proceeds.

questions.question: Which statement best contrasts bagging and boosting?
options: Both train models sequentially on reweighted data
Bagging trains models independently in parallel and averages them; boosting trains models sequentially, each correcting the previous one's errors
Bagging always overfits; boosting never does
Boosting only works with decision trees of depth 1
questions.explanation: Bagging (e.g. random forests) trains many models independently on bootstrap samples and aggregates them to reduce variance. Boosting builds models sequentially, each focusing on the residual errors of the prior, reducing bias.

questions.question: In a probabilistic classifier, lowering the decision threshold from 0.5 toward 0 will generally:
options: Increase recall (more positives predicted) at the cost of precision
Increase both precision and recall equally
questions.explanation: A lower threshold labels more instances positive, catching more true positives (higher recall) but also producing more false positives (lower precision). Threshold choice trades off precision and recall.

questions.question: A key practical difference between K-Means and DBSCAN clustering is that:
options: K-Means requires you to specify the number of clusters k; DBSCAN discovers clusters by density and can label outliers as noise
DBSCAN requires k; K-Means does not
Both require the number of clusters in advance
Neither can handle more than two features
questions.explanation: K-Means needs k specified upfront and assumes roughly spherical clusters. DBSCAN groups points by density, automatically finds the number of clusters, handles arbitrary shapes, and marks low-density points as noise/outliers.

questions.question: The primary goal of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is to:
options: Add new features to improve accuracy
Reduce dimensionality by projecting data onto directions of maximum variance
questions.explanation: PCA finds orthogonal axes (principal components) capturing the most variance and projects data onto the top components, reducing dimensionality while preserving as much information as possible.

questions.question: In training a neural network, backpropagation is the process of:
options: Computing gradients of the loss with respect to each weight and propagating them backward to update the weights
questions.explanation: Backpropagation applies the chain rule to compute the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight, propagating error backward through the layers so an optimizer (e.g. gradient descent) can update the weights.

questions.question: Why do neural networks use non-linear activation functions (e.g. ReLU) between layers?
options: Without non-linearity, stacked layers collapse into a single linear transformation, unable to model complex patterns
To reduce the number of parameters
They are optional and rarely used
questions.explanation: Non-linear activations let the network approximate complex, non-linear functions. Without them, any stack of linear layers is equivalent to one linear layer, severely limiting representational power.

questions.question: In Keras, what role does the optimizer (e.g. Adam, SGD) play during training?
options: It updates the model's weights using the gradients to minimise the loss function
questions.explanation: The optimizer determines how weights are adjusted from the computed gradients to reduce the loss each step. The loss function measures error; the optimizer (Adam, SGD, etc.) decides the update rule and step size.

questions.question: In a Convolutional Neural Network, what is the main purpose of a pooling layer?
options: To downsample feature maps, reducing spatial size and providing translation invariance
questions.explanation: Pooling (e.g. max pooling) reduces the spatial dimensions of feature maps, lowering computation and parameters while making the representation more robust to small translations in the input.

questions.question: LSTMs were designed to address which problem of vanilla RNNs?
options: The vanishing/exploding gradient problem that prevents learning long-range dependencies
questions.explanation: Standard RNNs struggle to learn long-range dependencies because gradients vanish or explode over many time steps. LSTMs use gated cells (input, forget, output gates) and a cell state to preserve information over long sequences.

questions.question: In NLP, tokenization is the process of:
options: Splitting text into smaller units such as words or subwords for processing
questions.explanation: Tokenization breaks raw text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) that become the model's input units. It is a foundational preprocessing step before tagging, embedding, or modelling.

questions.question: When deploying an ML model, why is model serialization (e.g. with pickle or joblib) necessary?
options: To save a trained model to disk so it can be loaded and used for inference later without retraining
questions.explanation: Serialization persists the trained model (weights and structure) to a file, so a production service can load it and serve predictions without retraining. joblib is often preferred for large NumPy-backed scikit-learn models.

questions.question: After deploying a model, monitoring for 'model drift' means watching for:
options: Degrading prediction quality as incoming data distributions change over time
The size of the model file growing
questions.explanation: Model (data/concept) drift occurs when the live data distribution shifts away from the training distribution, silently degrading accuracy. Monitoring detects this so the model can be retrained or updated.

questions.question: In Python, which of the following is an immutable data type?
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable; lists, dicts, and sets are mutable. Only immutable, hashable types can be dictionary keys.

questions.question: Inside a loop, what does `break` do?
questions.explanation: `break` terminates the whole loop immediately, whereas `continue` only skips the current iteration.

questions.question: A variable assigned inside a function (without `global`) is:
questions.explanation: Names bound inside a function are local by default and not visible outside it.

questions.question: Which object can be used as a dictionary key?
questions.explanation: Keys must be hashable/immutable; a tuple qualifies, lists/dicts/sets do not.

questions.question: Polymorphism in OOP means:
options: Different classes responding to a common interface in their own way
questions.explanation: Polymorphism lets different types respond to the same call differently; encapsulation hides state and inheritance derives from a parent.

questions.question: When does a `finally` block execute?
questions.explanation: `finally` always runs, making it ideal for cleanup regardless of exceptions.

questions.question: NumPy broadcasting enables:
options: Element-wise operations on compatibly-shaped arrays without explicit loops
questions.explanation: Broadcasting stretches compatible shapes for vectorized element-wise math without loops.

questions.question: A pandas Series versus a DataFrame:
options: Series is a single labelled column; DataFrame is a 2-D table
questions.explanation: A Series is one-dimensional; a DataFrame is a 2-D table of columns sharing an index.

questions.question: One-hot encoding is used to:
options: Convert categorical variables into binary indicator columns
questions.explanation: It turns categories into binary columns so models can consume them without implying false ordering.

questions.question: A p-value is:
options: The probability the null is true
The probability of data at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null is true
questions.explanation: It measures how extreme the data are under the null hypothesis; a small value casts doubt on the null.

questions.question: A Type I error is:
options: Failing to reject a false null
questions.explanation: Type I = false positive (rejecting a true null); Type II = false negative.

questions.question: In Matplotlib, a Figure:
options: Is the canvas that can hold one or more Axes
Is the same as an Axes
questions.explanation: The Figure is the top-level container; each Axes is a single plot within it.

questions.question: Lasso (L1) regularization is notable because it can:
options: Drive some coefficients exactly to zero (feature selection)
questions.explanation: L1 can zero out coefficients, selecting features; L2 (Ridge) shrinks but rarely zeroes them.

questions.question: The purpose of k-fold cross-validation is to:
options: Estimate performance reliably across multiple splits
questions.explanation: Rotating train/validation folds and averaging gives a robust performance estimate.

questions.question: A hyperparameter (versus a parameter) is:
options: Set before training to control the learning process
questions.explanation: Hyperparameters (learning rate, k, n_estimators) are set beforehand; parameters are learned during training.

questions.question: Bagging versus boosting:
options: Bagging trains models independently and averages; boosting trains sequentially correcting prior errors
questions.explanation: Bagging reduces variance via parallel independent models; boosting reduces bias via sequential error-correction.

questions.question: A key difference between K-Means and DBSCAN is:
options: K-Means needs k specified; DBSCAN finds clusters by density and labels noise
questions.explanation: K-Means requires k and assumes spherical clusters; DBSCAN uses density, finds cluster count, and flags outliers.

questions.question: PCA's primary goal is to:
options: Reduce dimensionality along directions of maximum variance
questions.explanation: PCA projects data onto top variance-capturing components to reduce dimensions while preserving information.

questions.question: Backpropagation is:
options: Computing loss gradients w.r.t. weights and propagating them backward to update them
questions.explanation: It uses the chain rule to compute gradients backward so an optimizer can update weights.

questions.question: Non-linear activation functions are needed because:
options: Without them stacked layers collapse into a single linear transform
questions.explanation: Non-linearities let networks model complex functions; without them depth adds no representational power.

questions.question: A CNN pooling layer mainly:
options: Downsamples feature maps, reducing size and adding translation invariance
questions.explanation: Pooling reduces spatial dimensions and adds robustness to small input shifts.

questions.question: LSTMs address which RNN problem?
questions.explanation: Gated LSTM cells preserve information over long sequences, mitigating vanishing/exploding gradients.

questions.question: Tokenization in NLP is:
options: Splitting text into words/subwords for processing
questions.explanation: It breaks text into token units that become the model's input.

questions.question: Model serialization (pickle/joblib) is needed to:
options: Persist a trained model to disk for later inference without retraining
questions.explanation: Serialization saves the trained model so a service can load and serve it without retraining.
